region_of_origin: Global (North America, Europe, New Zealand, Australia)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cooked venison is approximately 85g (3 oz), equivalent to a palm-sized portion of boneless cooked meat.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked deer/venison — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for cooked deer/venison (CR ID: F06080001). All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (1.2g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.76g/100g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.54g/100g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB #17162 (Deer, cooked, roasted) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate food
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cooked venison contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. The numeric value 0 is used as a sentinel to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index be numeric; gi_category is set to not_applicable to convey the true clinical interpretation. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition (GL = GI x carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 0).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (54mg/100g) with high potassium (335mg/100g) yielding a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio
hypertension.guidance: Cooked venison is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its low sodium content (54mg/100g) and high potassium (335mg/100g); however, patients should avoid high-sodium marinades, sauces, or cured venison products that can substantially increase sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate content with high protein (30.21g/100g) producing no glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cooked venison is an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, containing 0g carbohydrate and 30.21g protein per 100g, which produces no direct glycaemic response; patients should pair it with low-GI vegetables and avoid high-sugar glazes or marinades.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (3.19g/100g) and low saturated fat (1.2g/100g) with moderate cholesterol (112mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cooked venison is a lean red meat with low saturated fat (1.2g/100g), making it a heart-healthier alternative to beef or lamb for individuals with cardiovascular disease; however, the moderate cholesterol content (112mg/100g) warrants portion moderation in those with hypercholesterolaemia, and processed or cured venison products should be avoided.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acids) content in red game meat may amplify inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit cooked venison to no more than once per week, as its arachidonic acid content can amplify pro-inflammatory pathways; preference should be given to fatty fish (salmon, sardines) or plant proteins as primary protein sources, and venison should be paired with anti-inflammatory foods such as turmeric or omega-3-rich sides.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cooked deer meat, commonly known as venison, is a dark red game meat derived from various portions of deer (typically loin, leg, or shoulder), prepared without bone and cooked until tender by roasting, braising, stewing, or grilling. It has a firm, dense texture with a rich, distinctly gamey flavour that is more intense than beef or lamb. Venison is one of the leanest red meats available, containing only 3.19g of total fat and approximately 1.2g of saturated fat per 100g, while delivering an exceptionally high protein content of 30.21g per 100g and 158 kcal. It contains zero carbohydrate and zero sugar, making it suitable for ketogenic, low-carbohydrate, and diabetic-friendly dietary patterns. Its iron content (4.47mg/100g) is notably high, supporting haemoglobin synthesis and energy metabolism. Potassium (335mg/100g) and low sodium (54mg/100g) provide a favourable electrolyte profile for blood pressure management. Venison is consumed globally, particularly in North America, Europe, New Zealand, and Australia, and is increasingly popular in Singapore and Southeast Asia as a premium protein source. Clinically, it is well-suited for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease due to its lean profile, but individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should moderate intake due to its arachidonic acid content. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How much protein is in cooked deer meat?
Is deer meat low in fat?
Can I eat venison if I have high blood pressure?
What is the iron content of venison?
Is cooked deer meat suitable for heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F06080001) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, and polyunsaturated fat are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB #17162 (Deer, cooked, roasted), which is an approved secondary source and closely matches the HPB entry profile. All values are internally consistent.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as venison is a pure animal muscle protein with no carbohydrate content; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database and USDA FoodData Central. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal-derived food product with no plant fibre. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central NDB #17162). Saturated_fat_g (1.2g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.76g/100g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.54g/100g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB #17162 (Deer, cooked, roasted) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions; this is an approved secondary source adaptation. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed vitamin and mineral panel beyond those reported in HPB Foodo is not available in the HPB source for this specific entry; USDA FoodData Central NDB #17162 does report some of these values but they were not incorporated to avoid mixing source confidence levels across the full micronutrient panel without explicit cross-validation. Halal status is not confirmed as venison sourcing and slaughter method varies by supplier. Glycemic index is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index be numeric; gi_category is set to not_applicable to convey the true clinical interpretation, as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g carbohydrate per 100g) and GI is not clinically meaningful.

G6 is satisfied.
